title: Clean Water as a Basic Right (IELTS Band 8)

rounds.name: Round 1 · 连接词
rounds.description: 转折 / 并列 / 因果 / 对比 / 让步 / 总结

tokens.v: clean water is undoubtedly essential for human survival
should be recognised as a fundamental right, I do not believe this automatically means households should receive it completely free of charge.
governments should guarantee a basic amount of water for free,

charge for any usage beyond that.
Those who support free water supply have a compelling moral case. No one should be denied water
they cannot afford it,

in many developing regions, families still walk for hours to fetch water from unsafe sources. Making water free would ensure that even the poorest citizens can maintain hygiene, cook meals, and stay healthy. This would also reduce the spread of waterborne diseases
cholera,
remains a serious public health threat in parts of Africa and South Asia.
treating water as entirely free would create significant problems.
purifying, storing, and delivering water to every home is extremely expensive. The costs of pipelines, treatment plants, and maintenance must be covered by someone,
if users pay nothing, the burden falls entirely on taxpayers or the government budget.
free water tends to encourage waste.
people feel a resource has no value, they are far more likely to leave taps running, overwater their gardens,
ignore leaking pipes.
China,

water shortages affect hundreds of millions of people in the north, such behaviour would be disastrous.
A more balanced solution is a tiered pricing system. Each household could receive a free basic quota — enough for drinking, cooking, and washing —
heavier users pay progressively higher rates. This approach protects the poor, reflects water's true value,
encourages everyone to use it responsibly.
access to clean water is a basic right, providing it entirely free of charge is neither practical nor wise. Guaranteeing a free minimum supply
charging for additional use is a fairer and more sustainable policy.

rounds.name: Round 4 · 句法骨架
rounds.description: 整块从句 / 介词短语 / 修饰结构

tokens.answer: While clean water is undoubtedly essential for human survival

tokens.v: and should be recognised as a fundamental right, I do not believe

tokens.answer: this automatically means households should receive it completely free of charge.

tokens.v: In my view, governments should guarantee a basic amount of water for free,

tokens.answer: but charge for any usage beyond that.

tokens.v: Those
have a compelling moral case. No one should be denied water

tokens.answer: simply because they cannot afford it,

tokens.v: and in many developing regions, families still walk for hours

tokens.answer: to fetch water from unsafe sources.

tokens.v: Making water free would ensure

tokens.answer: that even the poorest citizens can maintain hygiene, cook meals, and stay healthy.

tokens.v: This would also reduce the spread of waterborne diseases

tokens.answer: such as cholera, which remains a serious public health threat in parts of Africa and South Asia.

tokens.v: However, treating water as entirely free would create significant problems. First,

tokens.answer: purifying, storing, and delivering water to every home

tokens.v: is extremely expensive. The costs of pipelines, treatment plants, and maintenance must be covered by someone, and

tokens.answer: if users pay nothing, the burden falls entirely on taxpayers or the government budget.

tokens.v: Second, free water tends to encourage waste.

tokens.answer: When people feel a resource has no value,

tokens.v: they are far more likely to leave taps running, overwater their gardens, or ignore leaking pipes. In countries like China,

tokens.answer: where water shortages affect hundreds of millions of people in the north,

tokens.v:
such behaviour would be disastrous.
A more balanced solution is a tiered pricing system. Each household could receive a free basic quota —

tokens.answer: enough for drinking, cooking, and washing
while heavier users pay progressively higher rates.

tokens.v:
This approach protects the poor, reflects water's true value, and encourages everyone to use it responsibly.
In conclusion,

tokens.answer: although access to clean water is a basic right,

tokens.v: providing it entirely free of charge is neither practical nor wise.

tokens.answer: Guaranteeing a free minimum supply while charging for additional use

tokens.v: is a fairer and more sustainable policy.
